articles.title: Surprise hike in international student visa application fees “a direct hit to Australia’s competitiveness”
articles.summary: Australia’s Department of Home Affairs announced an immediate, unconsulted hike in international student visa application fees for study, work, and working-holiday visas, effective 1 July 2026. The fee increase applies across all sub-sectors of the international education sector and is non-refundable.
articles.insight:
This fee hike signals potential downward pressure on Australia’s attractiveness for cost-sensitive international students, particularly from price-sensitive markets like India and Nigeria, though actual enrolment impact depends on concurrent scholarship offerings, exchange rates, and comparative fees in competing destinations.  
Admissions teams should track real-time application volume trends from key source countries to Australia, monitor competitor messaging around affordability, and assess whether partner agents are shifting counselling emphasis toward lower-cost alternatives or value-added support packages.

articles.title: ICEF Podcast: “Good, steady, and disciplined”: New Zealand’s plan for sustainable international enrolment growth
articles.summary: The ICEF Podcast features hosts Craig Riggs and Martijn van de Veen discussing recent developments in international student recruitment, including new foreign enrolment figures from the Netherlands and Azerbaijan’s efforts to expand its international student base. The episode then shifts to a live discussion with ICEF’s Rod Hearps about New Zealand’s strategy for achieving sustainable international enrolment growth, described as “good, steady, and disciplined.”
articles.insight:
New Zealand’s “good, steady, and disciplined” approach signals a deliberate pivot toward quality and sustainability over rapid growth, which may indirectly influence global perceptions of student mobility—though direct impacts on US, UK, or Australian demand remain uncertain without parallel policy shifts in those markets.  
Admissions teams should monitor New Zealand’s visa processing timelines, post-study work rule refinements, and source-market-specific enrolment trends, particularly in Asia and the Pacific, to assess whether its model inspires similar recalibrations elsewhere or creates new competitive opportunities.
